![ads icon](/styles/img/transparent_logo.svg)

# **ads**

### view

## ADS

## The historical evolution and research trends of life cycle assessment

#### Abstract

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a widely used tool for environmental decision-making; however, it still has theoretical and practical limitations. Through a comprehensive review of traditional LCA development and case studies, this study examines the overall trajectory of the evolution of the LCA methodological framework. It specifically addresses perspectives on typical LCA methods, dynamic LCA methods, expanding LCA into multidimensional assessment, simplifying the methodological framework, and integrating with other methods. Furthermore, it delves into improvements and optimizations of the methodological framework alongside their distinct characteristics. Drawing on insights from current analyses and the evolutionary path of the LCA methodological framework, this study outlines future research directions for LCA. It aims to serve as a reference for scholars in this field, thereby fostering further methodological enhancements and broadening the scope of LCA applications.

[10.1016/j.greenca.2024.08.003](/link_gateway/2024GrCar...2..425L/doi:10.1016/j.greenca.2024.08.003)

--- SOURCE 4: Recent advancements in prospective life cycle assessment ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155

SUMMARY:
Prospective Life Cycle Assessment (pLCA)1 plays a critical role as a tool to evaluate the future environmental performance of emerging technologies (Cucurachi et al., 2023). Unlike traditional LCA, which focuses on well-established systems based on their current state, pLCA addresses the complexities of scaling emerging technologies to industrial levels and assessing their impacts in future scenarios (Bergerson et al., 2020, Cucurachi et al., 2018, Moni et al., 2020). Additionally, while the development of new LCIA methods is essential for certain emerging technologies, it is often excluded from the scope of pLCA studies due to feasibility constraints (van der Hulst et al., 2020) which represents a gap in integrating novel impact assessment methods with pLCA practices.

FULL CONTENT:
Recent advancements in prospective life cycle assessment: Current practices, trends, and implications for future research - ScienceDirect

Review article

Recent advancements in prospective life cycle assessment: Current practices, trends, and implications for future research
=========================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Anissa Nurdiawati, Basit A.Mir, Sami G.Al-Ghamdi

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.resenv.2025.100203](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.resenv.2025.100203 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2666916125000155&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •The study highlights state-of-the-art trends and challenges of prospective LCA. 
*   •Background changes affect climate impact with trade-offs in water, metals, toxicity. 
*   •Prospective LCI database needs more sectoral, technological and model coverage. 
*   •Lack of prospective CFs, spatiotemporal variability, impact interlinkage with climate change. 
*   •Transparently articulating nuanced scenario development with uncertainties is crucial. 

Abstract
--------

Prospective Life Cycle Assessment (pLCA) is gaining interest due to its inherent future-oriented feature, which is an essential component of every decision-oriented life cycle assessment. Previous studies have highlighted challenges in conducting pLCA for emerging technologies, categorizing them into issues of comparability, data availability, scaling, and uncertainty and propose general frameworks to address these challenges. Accordingly, the application of pLCA is rapidly growing in recent years, with emerging methods addressing the limitations, and improving pLCA. This review study aims to compile and analyze emerging pLCA methods from scientific literature, identifying best practices and limitations to guide future research. It discusses methodological advancements in pLCA, including prospective life cycle inventory (pLCI) database, foreground modeling, scenario development and prospective life cycle impact assessment, offering insights for practitioners. While changes in background systems are increasingly addressed in pLCA studies, some, particularly earlier or less systematic ones, fall short of fully integrating nuanced future scenarios. The reviewed studies highlight that incorporating future scenarios related to the transformation of energy, material, transport, and industrial systems can significantly influence LCA outcomes, reinforcing the importance of explicitly integrating such scenarios into pLCA to ensure reliable and meaningful results. To ensure robust LCA studies, it is important to consider the use of pLCI databases, accounting for varying technology maturity levels, their improvement and diffusion rate, and incorporating spatial considerations. Yet, integrating pLCI databases with standard LCA tools remains complex, with a lack of practitioner guidance. Moreover, the interlinkage between climate change and various impact categories is a key source of uncertainty in future assessments, highlighting the need to improve both prospective inventory modeling and impact assessment. The findings call for future research to further explore the spatiotemporal effect of climate change on pLCA quantification, developing future-oriented characterization factors, expanding pLCI databases, as well as enhancing the applicability of pLCA studies through the integration of new analytical tools and models.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000313)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000362)

Keywords
--------

Life cycle assessment

Emerging technology

Prospective

Future scenario

Methodological framework

Integrated models

1. Introduction
---------------

To achieve set climate goals, transitioning to a more sustainable economy through the widespread adoption of cleaner technologies is crucial. While these technologies can be at various stages in their development cycle, ranging from basic ideas, lab prototypes, proof-of-concept, and industrial pilots to the commercial stage, notably many of these technologies are still in the development phase ([IEA, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b64)). Understanding the environmental impacts of emerging technologies from the early stages of development through to wide deployment is crucial to guide technology improvements, inform strategic decisions, and ensure they genuinely contribute to sustainability goals by reducing environmental impacts. Prospective Life Cycle Assessment (pLCA)[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fn1) plays a critical role as a tool to evaluate the future environmental performance of emerging technologies ([Cucurachi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b35)). Unlike traditional LCA, which focuses on well-established systems based on their current state, pLCA addresses the complexities of scaling emerging technologies to industrial levels and assessing their impacts in future scenarios ([Bergerson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b13), [Cucurachi et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b34), [Moni et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b86)). PLCA models emerging technology (i.e., technology that in the developmental stage and are not yet market ready ([Buyle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23))) as if they are in a projected mature phase, incorporating elements such as technology upscaling, proxy data utilization, experiential learning improvements, and various scenarios that consider changes in foreground and background systems relevant to future industrial production ([Arvidsson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b9), [Cucurachi et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b34)). However, pLCA can also be used to evaluate the future environmental impact of technology development, technological learning and the diffusion of more mature technologies using scenario modeling ([Buyle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23), [Mendoza Beltran et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b84), [Thonemann et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b137)).

The concept of technology maturity is especially critical in pLCA. Technology Readiness Levels (TRLs) provide a standardized framework to assess the maturity of a technology, ranging from the observation of basic principles (TRL 1) to a fully operational system in real-world conditions (TRL 9) ([NASA, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b92)). This progression is often described qualitatively in pLCA studies using terms like ‘lab scale’, ‘pilot scale,’ ‘large scale,’ and ‘commercial scale,’ which offer a more intuitive sense of development stages. When conducting an LCA in the early stages of research, primary concerns arise due to high uncertainties about the future performance of the technology at maturity and its background system. These challenges encompass factors such as scaling-up complexities, the absence of detailed data on raw materials, energy use, usage phase, and end-of-life phase, as well as uncertainties related to shifts in market, technology, or policy landscapes.

While pLCA is a widely used term, literature uses various other terms to describe these future-oriented LCAs, including anticipatory ([Ganesan and Valderrama, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b52), [Grimaldi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b57), [Wender et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b157)), ex-ante LCA ([Buyle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23), [Cucurachi et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b34)) and predictive LCA ([Karka et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b69)). Ex-ante and prospective LCA are closely related, with the key distinction that ex-ante LCA typically focuses on the future maturation of technology. Anticipatory LCA, similar to ex-ante LCA in modeling immature technologies at a future mature state, goes further by incorporating social dimensions through the active involvement of diverse stakeholders in methodological decisions, including defining system boundaries, selecting functional units, and determining impact categories and their weighting ([Arvidsson et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b8), [Wender et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b157)). The term “predictive LCA” is distinctly defined as a method that utilizes existing data from similar technologies to train regression models for forecasting the environmental impacts of technologies at low maturity ([Karka et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b69)).

However, many studies have highlighted ongoing inconsistencies in both terminology and methodology of these future-oriented LCA ([Bergerson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b13), [Bruhn et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b22), [Buyle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23), [Thonemann et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b137)). [Arvidsson et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b8) clarified the distinctions between future-oriented LCA concepts by focusing on three key dimensions of LCA studies: real-time, technology maturity, and causality, and provided recommendations for adopting consistent terminology. They also suggested that the distinctions between prospective and ex-ante LCA are considerably minor, for the sake of clarity in communication, they recommended harmonizing the terminology by using the term ‘prospective’ in addition to clearly describing the maturity level, among other relevant attributes, of the technology being analyzed.

Over time, the importance of pLCA in evaluating emerging technologies has been acknowledged, leading to the introduction of several detailed guidelines and frameworks tailored for this purpose. [Arvidsson et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b9) refined the concept of pLCA by focusing on early-stage technologies, assessing them through simulations of their future environmental impacts at scale and emphasizing the importance of dynamic modeling of background systems. [Cucurachi et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b34) define ex-ante LCA, outlining its specific features such as functionality, data, and impact assessment. They also discuss challenges in applying it to emerging technologies, using a literature-screening approach, though they acknowledge the selection of case studies was not exhaustive. [Buyle et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23) distinguish their work by presenting an ex-ante LCA framework that covers a technology’s full lifecycle, facilitating in-depth analysis of development, learning, and diffusion, and detail techniques for scaling emerging technologies to industrial levels. Similarly, [van der Hulst et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b145) developed an explicit framework for performing pLCA that emphasizes scenario analysis, expert involvement, and technology maturation (e.g., scaling and learning) as key elements, including a demonstration of its application in assessing future impacts. Yet, despite progress in systematizing pLCA, the adoption of this systematic frameworks in pLCA studies has been relatively limited and non uniforms ([Buyle et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b23)).

Several reviews have been published on pLCA, each addressing specific aspects of the field. [van der Giesen et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b144) performed a critical review, and identified inconsistent terminology in future-oriented LCA for emerging technologies as a major barrier to structured meta-analysis. [Thonemann et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b137) reviewed 65 papers, highlighting challenges and proposing frameworks for conducting pLCA, while [Bruhn et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b22) conducted a systematic literature screening to explore pLCA’s implications in the built environment, improve documentation and credibility, and identify practical tools to make pLCA more accessible to practitioners. [Tsoy et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b138) reviewed 18 studies on upscaling methods used in ex ante life cycle assessment of emerging technologies and provided decision trees. Similarly, [Erakca et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b46) focused on scaling techniques in 78 LCA-related studies, with background system changes falling outside the scope of their review. Additionally, while the development of new LCIA methods is essential for certain emerging technologies, it is often excluded from the scope of pLCA studies due to feasibility constraints ([van der Hulst et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b145)) which represents a gap in integrating novel impact assessment methods with pLCA practices.

Our work takes a holistic perspective by exploring the prospective approach within the four main stages of the LCA framework. What is considered as pLCA and selected in this study follows the harmonized definition proposed by [Arvidsson et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#b8): “_LCA that models the product system at a future point in time relative to the time at which the study is conducted”._ We review pLCA studies conducted over the past two decades, focusing on scaling emerging technologies to industrial levels, as well as those evaluating mature technologies under future scenarios, considering changes in both the specific technology under study (foreground) and the broader technological context (background systems). Drawing from an analysis of 328 articles and 16 methodological papers, we provide a comprehensive overview of trends, progress, and advancements across life cycle stages in LCA frameworks, including goal and scope definition, foreground and background system modeling (pLCI), and prospective life cycle impact assessment (pLCIA). Additionally, this review identifies research gaps and suggests direction for future studies to advance pLCA and support its broader application.

2. Material and methods
-----------------------

The study employs a semi-systematic approach, combining bibliometric data analysis and narrative review, to provide both a high-level overview and targeted insights into the field of pLCA. The bibliometric analysis focuses on understanding “how the field of pLCA has evolved” by mapping broad, high-level trends. Building on these bibliometric findings, the narrative review explores specific advancements, gaps, and challenges in the field. The review protocol is shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fig1).

The data analysis was guided by research questions presented in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fig1), which aim to identify the development and advancement of pLCA across the four key stages of the LCA framework.

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (727KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (727KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Review protocol of the study.

The review framework shown in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fig2) includes four steps to ensure a transparent and rigorous methodology.

During the planning phase, the research scope, research questions, inclusion and exclusion criteria, and databases were defined. The research utilized Scopus and Web of Science for database searches. The filter criteria applied were: (1) Language: English, (2) Document types: limited to full-text articles, or journal papers, (3) Time frame: the selected studies were published between the years 2000 and 2024 to ensure the relevance and capture current and emerging methodologies in the pLCA domain.

![Image 13](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (577KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (577KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Framework for final database selection, including literature collection, inclusion and exclusion criteria and paper count.

The search terms used included search phrases such as “emerging technolog*” which were paired with environmental assessment-related terms like “life cycle assessment”. The search strings included: (“life cycle assessment” OR “life cycle analysis”) AND (prospective OR “emerging technology” OR ex-ante OR “future scenario” OR “early-stage technology” OR “technology scaling” OR “novel product” OR “lab-scale technology”). In this identification stage, 1,363 articles were retrieved.

In the next stage, 493 duplicate articles were excluded after being identified in both databases based on matching titles. During the screening stage, the remaining records were thoroughly reviewed according to their titles and abstracts. Studies were included if they explicitly applied pLCA or contributed to its methodological development. Eligible studies modeled technologies, products, or systems at a future point in time, incorporating scenarios, projections, or upscaling approaches beyond the current state. Both case-study-based and methodological papers were considered, particularly those exploring modeling techniques, background system integration, and scenario development within pLCA frameworks. To ensure a comprehensive assessment of pLCA developments, we applied broad inclusion criteria while maintaining minimal exclusion filters. This approach allows the capture of methodological diversity, identifies inconsistencies in terminology and application, and reduces potential bias from overly restrictive selection criteria. A total of 542 records were excluded at this stage for meeting the exclusion criteria:

![Image 14](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr3.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (224KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr3_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (224KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666916125000155-gr3.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 3. Major aspects within the LCA methodological framework of the researched articles analyzed in this study.

*   •Irrelevance to the research questions (e.g., focusing on topics outside the scope of pLCA) 
*   •“Lab-Scale LCA” that assess immature technologies within the present timeframe, modeling them at approximately the same time as when the LCA is conducted. For example, LCA studies that only evaluate an early stage technology without upscaling scenarios or consideration of future timeframe. If abstracts lack sufficient detail, the methodology section is reviewed for clarity. 
*   •Irrelevant review papers: Review articles that are purely methodological, unrelated or only tangentially related to pLCA, or too broad in focus are excluded from the selection. 

This process narrowed the dataset to 328 research articles and 16 review articles for further analysis. The 328 studies were selected for in-depth analysis focusing on the applied LCA methodologies to examine how pLCA has evolved and been represented in the literature (Section [3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#sec3)). The snowball approach was chosen to complement the initial literature search and to deepen the exploration of specific topics within pLCA frameworks, novel methods, and pLCIA, resulting in the inclusion of 35 additional papers, as traditional keyword searches may miss critical studies due to variations in terminology or indexing. The comprehensive reference list, including that identified through the snowballing process can be found in the Supplementary Information (SI).

[Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fig3) illustrates the major categories within the LCA methodological framework used in the articles analyzed in this study. The figure provides a structured approach for analyzing the selected papers.

3. Analysis of research trends & developments
---------------------------------------------

This section presents analysis of research trends in pLCA, including both general study characteristics (Section [3.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#sec3.1)) and specific LCA attributes (Section [3.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#sec3.2)) from the selected literature.

### 3.1. General characteristics

[Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666916125000155#fig4) shows the evolution of the number of relevant publications, comprising a total of 328 case study articles examined in this review, categorized by their specific areas of LCA application. The analysis of publication trends over time reveals a growing interest in the application of pLCA to assess the future environmental impacts of technologies at different maturity stages, ranging from early stage to fully developed, under vari

enriched_points.expanded_content: The transition from static to dynamic LCA assessments reflects a growing emphasis on real-time data integration and predictive modeling for emerging technologies.

LCA has significantly advanced sustainability efforts by providing comprehensive frameworks for evaluating environmental impacts, guiding industries towards more sustainable practices and decision-making.

Industries encounter challenges in data availability, technological maturity, and methodological inconsistencies, hindering effective implementation of LCA frameworks in emerging technology assessments.
